title: Emergency Guide: Cat Convulsing and Foaming at the Mouth
meta_desc: If your cat is convulsing and foaming at the mouth, this is a high-priority emergency. Learn what to do immediately and how to seek urgent vet care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Seizures and foaming at the mouth in cats are critical medical emergencies that require immediate veterinary care. Providing a video of the event, if safe to capture, helps your veterinarian quickly diagnose the cause and provide life-saving treatment.

What is happening when my cat has a seizure and foams at the mouth?

When a cat experiences convulsions, it is often undergoing a seizure, which is a sudden surge of electrical activity in the brain. This event causes several physical reactions:

  • Involuntary muscle contractions and paddling of the limbs.
  • Loss of consciousness.
  • Hypersalivation (foaming at the mouth) caused by an inability to swallow or rapid jaw movements churning saliva into a froth.

Is it a medical emergency if my cat is having convulsions?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is a critical medical emergency. While a single, short seizure may not be immediately fatal, it is often a symptom of life-threatening conditions:

  • Toxin ingestion (such as lilies or antifreeze).
  • Head trauma or organ failure.
  • Neurological disorders.
  • Continuous seizures (status epilepticus) that can lead to brain damage or fatal hyperthermia.

How does a video or photo of the event help the veterinarian triage my cat?

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the clinic, capturing a quick video is incredibly helpful for the following reasons:

  • Seizures can look very similar to other conditions like fainting (syncope) or severe tremors.
  • Seeing the specific physical movements and consciousness levels helps the vet determine the type of neurological event.
  • A video allows the vet to select the most effective emergency treatment immediately upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In a convulsing cat foaming at the mouth, treatment should be aimed at controlling seizures, preventing asphyxiation, and providing supportive care. Stabilize the animal before decontamination if a toxin is suspected. Diazepam at 0.5-1 mg/kg, PO, bid, may be used but warrants close monitoring for hepatic necrosis, particularly in the first few weeks of therapy. Phenobarbital is the anticonvulsant of choice in cats, and levetiracetam (10-20 mg/kg, PO, tid) can be added if seizures are not well controlled. Injectable diazepam (1 mg/kg) can be given rectally to prevent trips to emergency clinics. KBr is not recommended in cats as it may cause asthma.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3171)

faqs.question: What should I do while my cat is convulsing?
faqs.answer: Keep your hands away from the cat's mouth to avoid accidental bites. Move furniture away to prevent injury, turn off bright lights, and keep the environment quiet. Do not attempt to restrain the cat.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat any medication at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human or leftover pet medications during a seizure, as this can worsen the condition or cause choking. Only a veterinarian should administer anti-convulsants.

faqs.question: How long do these episodes usually last?
faqs.answer: A typical seizure lasts between 30 seconds to 2 minutes. If a seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes, it is a life-threatening crisis requiring immediate intervention to stop the cycle.
